//: config/mcpo_config.json — JARVIS (CofCITIP) mcpo proxy, Phase 2 Session 4.
//format: mcpo uses Claude-Desktop-format JSON with an 'mcpServers' map. Each key becomes a REST route prefix (here: /jarvis-readonly) with its own /docs and /openapi.json. Chosen JSON (not YAML) because that is mcpo's documented/native config format.
//correction: The session prompt assumed mcpo could wrap connector functions (intune.query_compliance, ...) directly. It cannot — mcpo wraps an MCP *server* (a stdio command), not bare Python callables. So this points at tools/mcp_readonly_server.py, a thin FastMCP stdio server that registers those same read-only functions. Net effect is identical (those five tools as REST), done the way mcpo actually works.
//scope: READ-ONLY tools ONLY: intune.query_compliance, intune.query_device_detail, jamf.query_fleet, jamf.query_patch_status, servicenow.search_kb. NO write actions (create_ticket/update_ticket/create_incident/package_pipeline/teams.send_approval_request) — those stay behind jarvis_core's human confirmation gate. Do not add write tools to this server.
//roadmap: This is the on-ramp to exposing Microsoft Graph / ServiceNow / Teams as proper MCP servers later (Phase 2+). This session just proves the wrapping pattern on tools that already exist. mcpo is bound to loopback/campus LAN and key-protected in the systemd unit — this REST surface carries ops data and must never be off-campus reachable.

jarvis-readonly.//: stdio MCP server. cwd is the install dir so `from tools import ...` resolves; JARVIS_CONFIG points the connectors at the live config.env (mock honored). PYTHONPATH set for the same reason.
